title: Combustion Dynamics of Turbulent Swirling Flames
text:
Combustion Dynamics of Turbulent Swirling Flames
C. KU¨LSHEIMER* and H. BU¨CHNER
Lehrstuhl und Bereich Verbrennungstechnik am Engler-Bunte-Institut, Universita¨t Karlsruhe (TH),
Karlsruhe, Germany
In the present paper the inﬂuence of a periodic excitation of the mass ﬂow rate, varied in frequency and in
amplitude, on the isothermal ﬂow ﬁeld and on the ﬂame characteristics of swirl burners with different swirl
intensities S0,th is investigated. At ﬁrst the ﬂuid dynamical conditions for the formation of ring-vortex structures
in the burner near ﬂow ﬁeld are determined. The results indicate that the minimum level of excitation of the
mass ﬂow rate for vortex formation decreases hyperbolically with increasing frequency of pulsation and
characteristic Strouhal number, respectively. In further studies, the dynamical behavior of lean-premixed swirl
ﬂames is investigated, whereas the fuel gas/air mixture mass ﬂow rate at the burner exit was modulated
sinusoidally. The dynamical behavior of the investigated ﬂames is found to be dominated by two different
effects in certain frequency ranges: For moderate pulsation frequencies (fpuls  50 Hz) the detected periodic
heat release rate of pulsated, premixed swirl ﬂames is dominated by an effect that inhibits the strong
entrainment of ambient medium in comparison with the corresponding quasi steady-state swirl ﬂames. With
increasing frequency (fpuls  50 Hz) this effect will be overlaid by the periodical formation of ring-vortices
entraining additional ambient medium and interfering with the combustion process. The physical understanding
of the frequency-dependent ﬂame dynamics (ﬂame transfer function) on periodic disturbances is indispensable
for the prediction of the formation of combustion-driven oscillations that occur in technical combustion systems
(e.g., gas turbines, industrial combustors). It is also evident for the development of methods to prevent or
suppress periodic combustion instabilities. The results of the ﬂame transfer measurements presented in this
paper will lead to a basic understanding of the formation and reaction of large-scale coherent vortex structures
in turbulent ﬂames, that are well known as drivers of combustion instabilities.

title: Combustion Dynamics of Turbulent Swirling Flames
text:
INTRODUCTION
The development of combustion systems to
minimize the ﬂue gas emissions (NOx, CO, HC,
etc.) and to increase the system’s efﬁciency (e.g.,
gas turbine) is combined with technical modiﬁ-
cations or new combustor design. This also
includes modiﬁcations of the combustion pro-
cess using premixed systems and highly turbu-
lent swirl stabilized ﬂames. The application of
these modiﬁcations makes high-intensity com-
bustion and therewith, combustion systems with
higher reaction densities possible. Increasing
stability problems caused by the appearance of
combustion-driven oscillations are observed to
be linked with these improvements. The in-
duced pressure oscillations can lead to the total
damage of the installations and therefore, the
manufacturers are forced to operate these com-
bustors under not acceptable conditions. The
best way to develop measures to suppress or to
eliminate these combustion driven oscillations
is, in contrast to time- and cost-intensive trial
and error solutions, to realize and to understand
the relevant feedback mechanism, that is neces-
sary to sustain the periodic pressure oscillations
in the combustion chamber.
Possible feedback mechanisms for the sus-
tainment of combustion driven oscillations in
highly turbulent, premixed combustion systems
are schematically illustrated in Fig. 1. As a very
important mechanism driving these combustion
instabilities the in-phase formation and combus-
tion of large-scale coherent vortical structures
were identiﬁed [1–5]. Another important mech-
anism considers the role of equivalence ratio
ﬂuctuations caused by the pressure oscillations
coupling with the mixing unit in industrial com-
bustion systems. For a detailed description of
that mechanism we refer to literature [6–8].
This mechanism doesn’t play a role in the
current experiments, as the equivalence ratio
was kept constant, ensuring a homogeneous,
perfect mixture during the ﬂame experiments.
The formation of ring-vortex structures is a
fundamental ﬂuid dynamical phenomenon, that
can always be observed together with the occur-
rence of technical relevant, not tolerable pres-
sure oscillations in highly turbulent combustors.
The appearance of these ring-vortices was
found for non-premixed as well as for premixed
combustion systems and is also independent of
the applied ﬂame type (jet-, bluff body-, or swirl
stabilized ﬂames). At a particular combination
of amplitude and frequency of the pressure
ﬂuctuations (pˆdis, fdis) these toroidal vortices are
formed, rolling-up the outer regions of the fuel
gas/air mixture ﬂow together with additional
entrained ambient medium [2, 9–11]. There-
fore, the mixing and reaction ﬁelds of the
steady-state ﬂame are changing totally. The
reaction of the fuel in the vortex causes a
ﬂuctuation of the heat release rate of the ﬂame.
The resulting ﬂuctuation of the ﬂue gas produc-
tion rate leads to a ﬂuctuation of the static
pressure in the combustion chamber (Fig. 1). In
the case that the pressure ﬂuctuations p˜cc(t) in
the combustion chamber are in-phase with the
ﬂuctuations of the heat release rate of the
ﬂame, fulﬁlling Rayleigh’s criterion [12], and
are large enough to cover friction losses of the
combustor, the ﬂuctuation of mass ﬂow rate at
the burner exit ˜m˙ mix can be ampliﬁed. This
ampliﬁcation of the ﬂow rate oscillation can
cause a next larger ring-vortex generation, con-
taining a larger share of the fuel gas/air mixture
(feedback loop), which in turn ampliﬁes the
ﬂuctuation of the heat release rate and of the
static pressure in the combustion chamber. This
feedback loop clearly explains a mechanism for
the ampliﬁcation of initial small disturbances to
high-amplitude pressure oscillations at proper
timing conditions.
Hence, the understanding of the interaction
of the periodic ﬂow ﬁeld, forced by the pressure
ﬂuctuations in the combustion chamber, and the
71
DYNAMICS OF TURBULENT SWIRLING FLAMES

ﬂame response is one of the keys to ﬁnd meth-
ods to control self-excited combustion instabil-
ities sustained by this mechanism. Investigations
to determine the transfer functions of ﬂames
are based on two important aspects, that have
direct inﬂuence on the appearance of periodic
combustion instabilities in a burner-ﬂame-com-
bustion chamber assembly (Fig. 1):
1. The ﬂame provides the energy for the ampli-
ﬁcation of initial small disturbances in the
combustion system and for the sustainment
of the pressure ﬂuctuations, while it covers
the friction losses of the ﬂue gas oscillation in
the combustion chamber.
2. The effective time delay between the distur-
bance of the mass ﬂow through the burner
nozzle and the ﬂuctuation of the resulting
heat release rate of the ﬂame varies in de-
pendence on numerous operation parame-
ters of the ﬂame (e.g., thermal power, air
equivalence ratio, air-preheating). Whereas,
in a deﬁned burner and combustion chamber
geometry, the time delay between heat re-
lease rate and pressure ﬂuctuation and be-
tween pressure and mass ﬂow modulation
are more or less ﬁxed at a given geometry of
the components. Therefore, the ﬂame is the
most variable component with regard to its
ﬂexibility in closing the feedback loop.
In the present paper the formation of the
large-scale toroidal vortices in isothermal swirl
ﬂows is determined in dependence of frequency
and amplitude of a harmonically modulated
mass ﬂow rate at the nozzle exit. The inﬂuence
of the vortical structures on the combustion
process is discussed by means of measuring
ﬂame transfer functions as it was done in pre-
vious papers [4, 5]. In analogy to control theory
[13] the ﬂame transfer function describes the
dynamic response characteristics of the ﬂame in
form of an amplitude response Fﬂ(fpuls) and a
phase angle function ﬂ(fpuls). Therefore, the
time functions of the ﬂame excitation and of the
resulting ﬂame response have to be determined.
In this paper the time signals correlated with the
mixture mass ﬂow rate at the burner exit (ﬂame
excitation) and with the heat release rate of the
entire ﬂame (ﬂame response) were recorded
simultaneously and analyzed to calculate the
ﬂame transfer function.
Fig. 1. Feedback mechanisms in technical combustion systems.
72
C. KU¨ LSHEIMER AND H. BU¨ CHNER

The amplitude response Fﬂ(fpuls) is given by
the ratio of the rms-value of the total heat
release rate and the rms-value of the mixture
mass ﬂow rate at the burner exit at the adjusted
frequency of pulsation fpuls, normalized by their
respective rms-values of the corresponding qua-
si-steady ﬂame (here: at fpuls  fref  5 Hz). The
phase angle function Fl(fpuls) is related to the
overall time delay td,ﬂ(fpuls) between the peri-
odic ﬂuctuations of the fuel gas/air mixture ﬂow
at the nozzle exit and the resulting periodic
ﬂuctuation of the reaction rate of the entire
turbulent ﬂame.

title: Combustion Dynamics of Turbulent Swirling Flames
text:
EXPERIMENTAL SETUP AND
MEASUREMENT TECHNIQUES
The investigations of pulsated isothermal ﬂow
ﬁelds and natural gas-ﬁred, premixed swirl
ﬂames were carried out by using swirl burners
with different swirl intensities S0,th  0.21, 0.45
and 0.79. The swirl intensities S0,th were calcu-
lated from the geometric data of the axial vane
swirlers (db  57 mm, dbluff-body  35 mm) with
different vane angles (vane  15, 30, and 45
degree). The calculation equation for S0,th is
given in [14]. Figure 2 shows schematically the

title: Combustion Dynamics of Turbulent Swirling Flames
text:
experimental setup and the applied measure-
ment techniques.
An adjustable part of the total fuel gas/air
mixture mass ﬂow was modulated nearly sinu-
soidally in time using a pulsating unit. This
pulsating unit can generate periodic oscillations
of the mass ﬂow rate at the burner nozzle exit
within a frequency range between 1 to 350 Hz
with continuously adjustable amplitudes up to
70% of the mean mass ﬂow rate. A detailed
description of this pulsating unit is given in
previous papers [4–6, 10]. The pulsation fre-
quency can be chosen by adjusting the rotation
of the stepping motor with an accuracy of 0.01
Hz. In the case of ﬂame investigations a decou-
pling chamber was positioned between the pul-
sating unit and the mixing section of the reac-
tants. This decoupling chamber prevents the
periodic pressure oscillations, generated by the
Fig. 2. Scheme of the experimental set-up and the applied measuring techniques.
73
DYNAMICS OF TURBULENT SWIRLING FLAMES

pulsating unit, from inﬂuencing the mass ﬂow
adjustment with the ﬂow meters and the fuel
gas/air mixing process [6]. Therewith, the equiv-
alence ratio of the fuel gas/air mixture mass ﬂow
through the burner was kept constant during all
ﬂame experiments.
The applied measurement techniques were:
●Hot-wire anemometry to determine the peri-
odic mass ﬂow ﬂuctuation at the burner exit
(ﬂame excitation).
●A photomultiplier detecting the overall radi-
ation intensity of the ﬂame (ﬂame response)
at a wavelength   306.7 nm, emitted by
excited OH*-radicals from the reaction zone
of the ﬂame. This wavelength corresponds to
the (0, 0) band of the 2  2 electronic state
transition of the diatomic OH molecule [15].
●Phase-correlated picture-taking of the period-
ically changing ﬂow ﬁeld or ﬂame structures.
Using a trigger signal, generated by an induc-
tive sensor, which is mounted in the pulsating
unit, phase-locked picture-taking at a ﬁxed
relative time ttr/tp within the period of excita-
tion is possible. An additional time delay unit
allows to observe the time history of the
periodically disturbed ﬂow ﬁeld and ﬂame
structure at different moments t/tp within the
period of excitation. To realize short exposure
times (texp  10–100 	s), that are necessary
for watching the ﬂow ﬁeld or the ﬂame struc-
ture at particular moments, an intensiﬁed
CCD-Camera (512  512 Pixel up to 16 bit
A/D at 1 MHz) was used (Fig. 2).
Determination of Fluid Dynamical Conditions
for Vortex Formation
The determination of the ﬂuid dynamical con-
ditions to generate ﬁrst-time coherent vortex
structures in a periodically modulated isother-
mal ﬂow ﬁeld was carried out by using an in situ
calibrated single tungsten hot-wire sensor (2
mm in length and 5 	m in diameter) located
between two vanes of the swirler at the burner
exit. With the help of the measured time-
dependent hot-wire signal voltage Uhw(t) the
mean value m˙ b and the rms-value m˙ b,rms of the
mass ﬂow rate m˙ b(t) at the burner exit can be
calculated. It has to be mentioned that this
calculation is based on some assumptions: con-
stant velocity proﬁle in radial direction at any
moment within the period of excitation, rota-
tional symmetry, incompressible ﬂow. Addition-
ally the rms-value includes to a small extent also
turbulent ﬂuctuations. The level of excitation
Pu, which is together with the pulsating fre-
quency fpuls the dominating parameter to de-
scribe the formation of coherent vortex struc-
tures in periodically disturbed ﬂow ﬁelds, can be
calculated using Eq. 1 [4–6]:
Pu

m˙ b
(1)
At the beginning of the investigations the mean
mass ﬂow rate m˙ b and the frequency fpuls were
adjusted. The rectangular part of the exit area
of the pulsating unit (bypass ﬂow) [4–6, 10] was
opened completely, whereas the amplitude of
the periodic excitation of the mixture mass ﬂow
was set to minimum. After that, the pulsation
level Pu was increased continuously by closing
the bypass area of the pulsating unit while the
frequency of pulsation fpuls was kept constant.
At the same time the ﬂow ﬁeld of the burner
near region (120–150 mm), visualized by the
means of a tracer medium and laser light-sheet
technique, has been observed. The critical pul-
sation level Pucrit was reached in the case that a
ﬁrst-time coherent vortex ring structure was
formed at any moment within the period of
disturbance. In this way the critical pulsation
level Pucrit, characterizing the appearance of
these toroidal structures, was determined for
different pulsation frequencies fpuls at burners
with different swirl intensities and at different
mean ﬂow rates m˙ b [16].
Determination of the Flame Transfer
Function
For the measurements of ﬂame transfer func-
tions (Fig. 2) the determination of the mean-
and rms-value of the pulsated ﬂow rate (ﬂame
excitation) was conducted again by hot-wire
anemometry. The position of the hot-wire was
located 15 mm upstream of the burner exit
between two vanes of the swirler. This was
necessary to protect the hot-wire sensor from
destruction caused by a ﬂame ﬂashback during
the pulsated ﬂame investigations. For the cap-
74
C. KU¨ LSHEIMER AND H. BU¨ CHNER

ture of the ﬂame response a photomultiplier
detecting the overall radiation of the excited
OH*-radicals at 306.7 nm was used. Because of
the strong dependence of this radiation inten-
sity Upm on the reaction rate Q˙ th and on the
air equivalence ratio premix (1/), because of
their inﬂuence on ﬂame geometry and ﬂame
temperature, respectively, their correlation was
calibrated using steady-state non-enclosed pre-
mixed swirl ﬂames.
In Fig. 3a the non-linear dependence of the
radiation intensity on the air equivalence ratio
premix is shown. The individual data were nor-
malized with the corresponding maximum value
of radiation intensity at stoichiometric mixture
of each thermal load level Q˙ th. The correlation
between the heat release rate and the air equiv-
alence ratio for different swirl intensities is
shown in Fig. 3b. The radiation intensity is
obviously lower for increasing swirl intensities.
This is caused by the strongly intensiﬁed en-
trainment of ambient air with increasing swirl
[14]. Therefore, the OH*-radiation intensities
measured for the swirl intensity S0,th  0.0 (vane
angle of the swirl generator equals zero) is the
maximum level of OH*-radiation of steady-
state “swirl” ﬂames with corresponding thermal
load and air equivalence ratio values. These
correlations are used later on for the physical
interpretation of the frequency-dependent reac-
tion behavior of pulsated swirl ﬂames.

experimental setup and the applied measure-

An adjustable part of the total fuel gas/air
mixture mass ﬂow was modulated nearly sinu-
soidally in time using a pulsating unit. This
pulsating unit can generate periodic oscillations
of the mass ﬂow rate at the burner nozzle exit
within a frequency range between 1 to 350 Hz
with continuously adjustable amplitudes up to
70% of the mean mass ﬂow rate. A detailed
description of this pulsating unit is given in
previous papers [4–6, 10]. The pulsation fre-
quency can be chosen by adjusting the rotation
of the stepping motor with an accuracy of 0.01
Hz. In the case of ﬂame investigations a decou-
pling chamber was positioned between the pul-
sating unit and the mixing section of the reac-
tants. This decoupling chamber prevents the
periodic pressure oscillations, generated by the

adjustment with the ﬂow meters and the fuel
gas/air mixing process [6]. Therewith, the equiv-
alence ratio of the fuel gas/air mixture mass ﬂow
through the burner was kept constant during all
ﬂame experiments.
The applied measurement techniques were:
●Hot-wire anemometry to determine the peri-
odic mass ﬂow ﬂuctuation at the burner exit
(ﬂame excitation).
●A photomultiplier detecting the overall radi-
ation intensity of the ﬂame (ﬂame response)
at a wavelength   306.7 nm, emitted by
excited OH*-radicals from the reaction zone
of the ﬂame. This wavelength corresponds to
the (0, 0) band of the 2  2 electronic state
transition of the diatomic OH molecule [15].
●Phase-correlated picture-taking of the period-
ically changing ﬂow ﬁeld or ﬂame structures.
Using a trigger signal, generated by an induc-
tive sensor, which is mounted in the pulsating
unit, phase-locked picture-taking at a ﬁxed
relative time ttr/tp within the period of excita-
tion is possible. An additional time delay unit
allows to observe the time history of the
periodically disturbed ﬂow ﬁeld and ﬂame
structure at different moments t/tp within the
period of excitation. To realize short exposure
times (texp  10–100 	s), that are necessary
for watching the ﬂow ﬁeld or the ﬂame struc-
ture at particular moments, an intensiﬁed
CCD-Camera (512  512 Pixel up to 16 bit
A/D at 1 MHz) was used (Fig. 2).

Investigations of the Flame Dynamics (Flame
Transfer Function)
Flame Transfer Functions of Axial Jet
Flames
For a better understanding of the ﬂame re-
sponse of premixed swirl ﬂames, the ﬂame
characteristics of pulsated premixed axial jet
ﬂames (no swirl) are discussed ﬁrst. In Fig. 5 the
amplitude response (Fig. 5a) and the phase
angle function (Fig. 5b) of turbulent, premixed
jet ﬂames are shown. These results are taken
from previous investigations [4, 5]. Introducing
a characteristic Strouhal number [4, 5] the dy-
namic jet ﬂame characteristics could be de-
scribed independent of mean thermal load
Q˙ th(Re) and encosure conditions. The ampli-
tude responses show that the dynamic jet ﬂame
characteristics can be divided into two signiﬁ-
cantly different regions. Up to a certain Strou-
hal number (Str  0.33 [4, 5]) the normalized
amplitude response F remains zero. This
means that the pulsated ﬂame behaves in every
moment within the period of excitation as the
steady-state ﬂame with the corresponding mean
thermal load Q˙ th and air equivalence ratio
premix, with regard to the OH*-radiation inten-
sity of the entire ﬂame. Therefore, this region of
Strouhal number (Str 
 0.33) is called “quasi
steady-state ﬂame” behavior, with regard to the
mixing ﬁeld and the reaction.
With increasing Strouhal number (Str 	 0.33)
the amplitude response decreases because of
the formation and reaction of coherent ring-
vortex structures [4, 5]. During their formation
the ring-vortices strongly mix in additional am-
bient medium. Therefore, the fuel gas rolled up
in the vortex is stronger diluted compared with
the fuel gas concentration in the corresponding
steady-state ﬂame. In the non-enclosed case the
dilution of the fuel gas share in the vortex with
additional ambient air causes an increase of the
local air equivalence ratio . Because of the
strong dependence of the OH*-radiation inten-
sity on the air equivalence ratio or ﬂame tem-
perature, as shown in Fig. 3a, this dilution leads
to a decrease of the measured photomultiplier
voltage Upm(t) during the vortex combustion in
comparison to the corresponding steady-state
ﬂame. Therewith, the rms-value of the ﬂame
response Upm,rms(fpuls) and the amplitude re-
sponse F(fpuls) (see Eq. 2) decrease [4, 5].
In Fig. 5a and b the ﬂame transfer functions
are compared with the theoretical model of an
ideal idle-time model, well known in control
theory [13]. This simple model is characterized
by the absence of any damping or ampliﬁcation
in the amplitude response F (here: Upm,rms
(fref)  Upm,rms(fpuls); ub,rms(fpuls)  const.) and
by a constant time delay for the phase angle
(td(fpuls)  const.). Up to the Strouhal number
of about 0.33, characterized by quasi steady-
state ﬂame behavior, a good agreement can be
observed between the measured amplitude re-
sponse and the model. The measured phase
angles show a smooth frequency dependence.
Their course is in a good agreement with the
theoretical model even for higher Strouhal
numbers. This agreement demonstrates that the
frequency-dependent phase angle of a jet ﬂame

quency-independent time delay. This time delay

reaction zone of the ﬂame (xOH,max) [4, 5]. With

Strouhal number, used in Figs. 5a and b, was

This additional entrainment of ambient air
leads to an increase of the local air equiva-

bined with a decrease of the local combus-
tion
temperature.
Therefore,
the
OH*-

(see Fig. 3b). An effect, that inhibits the
additional entrainment of swirl ﬂames during
certain moments within the period of excita-
tion, would cause the reaction of a particular
amount of fuel gas at lower local air equiva-

compared with the quasi steady-state ﬂame
(Upm,rms(fpuls) 	 Upm,rms(fref)) and there-

Furthermore, this interpretation model is
underlined by the results shown in Fig. 6a:
Increasing swirl intensity is correlated with
an increase of entrainment of ambient air in
the steady-state swirl ﬂame [14] and so, as
discussed above, with a decrease of the OH*-
radiation intensities (see Fig. 3b). A restric-
tion of entrainment in the pulsated case can
result in a further increase of the OH*-
radiation intensities related to the steady-
state OH*-emissions with increasing swirl
intensity. This is in good agreement with the
higher values of the amplitude response
measured at the pulsated swirl ﬂames with
higher swirl intensity.

a part of the fuel gas at lower local air
equivalence ratio than in the corresponding

moderate increase of the pulsation frequency
up to fpuls  50 Hz, while keeping

of the fuel gas/air mixture, reacting in a
region of hot temperature, could not ob-
served in the quasi steady-state case (Fig.
9a). This reaction at higher temperature and
possibly lower air equivalence ratio as in the
quasi steady-state ﬂame can therewith ex-
plain an increase of the amplitude response.
To strengthen the importance of this ef-
fect, described with the help of phase-corre-
lated images, we measured the ﬂame transfer
function of an enclosed pulsated swirl ﬂame.
The results are shown in Fig. 10. The ampli-
tude response of the enclosed case shows a
nearly quasi steady-state ﬂame characteristic
in the moderate frequency range (fpuls  50
Hz). This signiﬁcant difference between the
amplitude responses of non-enclosed and
enclosed pulsated swirl ﬂames can be ex-
plained with the reaction of a fuel gas share
in the region of the inner recirculation zone.
In contrast to the non-enclosed case the
reaction of the entire fuel gas of the enclosed
pulsated swirl ﬂame takes place in regions
with hot temperature (inner and outer recir-
culation zone) at any frequency of pulsation.
Therefore, the reaction of the particular
amount of fuel gas in the inner recirculation
zone (fpuls  50 Hz) does not amplify the
ﬂuctuation of the OH-radiation intensity in
comparison to the corresponding enclosed
quasi steady-state ﬂame (fref  5 Hz). The
measured small increase of the amplitude
response of the enclosed pulsated swirl ﬂame
could be caused by the fact, that the combus-
tion chamber was not perfectly insulated
(non-adiabatic). Therefore, temperature of
the outer recirculation zone

 Fﬂ(fpuls)max). At this frequency the inﬂuence
of periodically formed toroidal vortices on the
combustion process exceeds the contrary, en-
trainment-restricting effect (3fmax  fcrit).
The phase angle functions ﬂ(fpuls) of pulsated
premixed swirl ﬂames, displayed in Fig. 6b and
7b, differ considerably from the phase angle of
the ideal idle-time model. As mentioned before,
this model describes very well the phase angle
functions of pulsated premixed axial jet ﬂames
[4, 5] and is characterized by a frequency-
independent time delay (td,ﬂ(fpuls)  const.).
This means (see Eq. 3), that for pulsated pre-
mixed swirl ﬂames the time delay between the
ﬂuctuation of the mass ﬂow rate at the burner
exit and the resulting ﬂuctuation of the heat
release rate of the ﬂame is strongly frequency-
dependent (td,ﬂ(fpuls)  const.), mainly in the
low frequency range (fpuls 
 50 Hz). The devi-
ation from the ideal idle-time model seems to
be caused by the same effect that increases the
amplitude response of non-enclosed premixed
swirl ﬂames. This idea is underlined by the

title: Combustion Dynamics of Turbulent Swirling Flames
text:
results shown in Fig. 10. In the case of the
enclosed swirl ﬂame the phase angle shows a
very good agreement with the model for the low
frequency range (fpuls 
 50 Hz) in contrast to
the non-enclosed swirl ﬂame (Fig. 10b). In this
range of moderate pulsation frequency the am-
plitude response of the non-enclosed swirl ﬂame
increases signiﬁcantly, whereas the amplitude
response of the enclosed swirl ﬂame remains
nearly constant (Fig. 10a).
For higher swirl intensities the phase angles
decrease with a modest gradient and therewith,
the swirl ﬂame reacts on periodic excitation with
a shorter time delay than in the case of lower
swirl intensities (Fig. 6b). This dependency of
the phase angle on swirl intensity can be ex-
plained by the increasing of the reaction density,
resulting in a shorter ﬂame length with higher
swirl. Therefore, the mean convective time de-
lay for the transport of the mixture from the
burner nozzle to the main reaction zone of the
ﬂame is decreased with increasing swirl inten-
sity. Assuming that the convective time delay
dominates the total time delay occuring in this
premixed ﬂame system, the phase angle of a
pulsated premixed ﬂame decreases with increas-
ing swirl intensity.
For different pulsation levels the phase angle
functions ﬂ(fpuls) (Fig. 7b) show the same
frequency-dependent
behavior
of
premixed
non-enclosed swirl ﬂames (td,ﬂ(fpuls)  const.).
Though, there is no inﬂuence of the pulsation
level on the time delay and phase angle, respec-
tively. This result is very important for the
physical understanding of the formation and
ampliﬁcation mechanism of self-excited com-
bustion oscillations (see Fig. 1). The formation
and reaction of an initial small ring-vortex co-
herent to the pressure ﬂuctuation in the com-
bustion chamber can cause the growing of the
vortex and of the periodic part of the heat
release rate and therewith, the pressure oscilla-
tions will be ampliﬁed. At the same time the
phase angle between the oscillation of the mix-
ture mass ﬂow through the burner and the
oscillation of the heat release rate of the ﬂame
(ﬂ) remains constant. This is essential for the
in-phase interaction of the periodical distur-
bances inside the feedback cycle burner–ﬂame-
combustion chamber during the origin and
ampliﬁcation of self-sustained pulsating com-
bustion. If the ﬂame would change its phase
angle ﬂwith increasing pulsation level Pu, the
growth of combustion-driven pressure oscilla-
tions would inhibit itself.

title: Combustion Dynamics of Turbulent Swirling Flames
text:
CONCLUSIONS
The periodic formation and reaction of ring-
vortex structures is a fundamental phenomenon
combined with the appearance of self-excited
ﬂame-/pressure oscillations in technical com-
bustion systems. Therefore, it is very important
to get to know more details about the ﬂuid
dynamical conditions (fpuls, Pu, m˙ b) of the ring-
vortex formation in the isothermal ﬂow ﬁeld as
well as under combustion conditions. Further-
more, a better understanding of the ring-vortex
inﬂuence on the combustion process itself is
necessary. In the present paper the ﬂuid dynam-
ical conditions for the formation of coherent
ring-vortex structures in periodic modulated
isothermal swirl ﬂows are found to be deter-
mined by the combination of the dimensionless
parameters Pu (pulsating level) and Str (nor-
malized excitation frequency). The minimum
excitation level for vortex formation decreases
82
C. KU¨ LSHEIMER AND H. BU¨ CHNER

with increasing Strouhal number Str (hyperboli-
cal decline: Pucrit  1/Str).
Investigations of the frequency response of
pulsated premixed swirl ﬂames show two signif-
icant effects on their combustion process: The
increase of the amplitude response (ﬁrst effect)
of pulsated swirl ﬂames at moderate pulsation
frequencies, that was not observed in pulsated
premixed axial jet ﬂames, can be explained by
an effect, that inhibits the additional entrain-
ment of ambient medium in comparison with
the quasi steady-state premixed swirl ﬂames at
the same thermal load and air equivalence ratio.
Therefore, the dynamic characteristics of non-
enclosed premixed swirl ﬂames show obvious
differences to the jet ﬂame behavior. The de-
creasing amplitude response with further in-
creasing pulsation frequency represents the be-
ginning
inﬂuence
of
periodically
formed
toroidal vortices on the combustion process
(second effect). Therefore, the amplitude re-
sponses of the investigated non-enclosed pre-
mixed swirl ﬂames are characterized by maxima
with positive values at a particular frequency.
In analogy to the results found by isothermal
ﬂow investigations, the increase of the distur-
bance level Pu causes the formation of ring-
vortices at lower frequencies. Therewith, the
interfering of coherent vortex structures on the
combustion process starts at considerable lower
frequencies. However, the critical frequencies
for vortex formation in the isothermal swirl
ﬂows (Fig. 4) and for ﬂame-vortex interaction in
the ﬂame measurements (Fig. 6a and 7a) at a
constant pulsation level Pu are different. For
the isothermal swirl ﬂows the critical frequency
and Strouhal number, respectively, character-
izes the onset of vortex formation in the pul-
sated isothermal swirl ﬂows close to the burner
exit. In the ﬂame investigations the critical
frequency (maximum of the amplitude re-
sponse) characterizes the beginning of a notice-
able interaction of the ﬂuid dynamically con-
trolled vortex formation with the combustion
process.
The obtained phase angles represent fre-
quency-dependent time delays td,ﬂ(fpuls) of the
investigated swirl ﬂames on periodic excitation.
This characteristic time delay of the ﬂame con-
siderably inﬂuences the in-phase interaction of
periodical disturbances inside the feedback cy-
cle “burner-ﬂame-combustion chamber” (Fig.
1), which is necessary for coupling self-sustained
pulsating combustion. Therefore, the knowl-
edge of the phase angles of ﬂames in depen-
dence of Q˙ th, premix, S0,th, and so forth is
important for prediction models of the stability
limits (feedback coupling) of premixed combus-
tion systems.
The measured ﬂame transfer functions of
pulsated swirl ﬂames can be used to check and
to validate numerical simulations of the ﬂame
behavior for different excitation frequencies
and amplitudes. Additionally, the determina-
tion of the ﬂame dynamics is essential to im-
prove prediction models for the formation of
self-sustained combustion instabilities. Men-
tion, that there are no other experimental data
of periodically excited swirl ﬂames in literature
yet. Furthermore, the results obtained from the
ﬂame
response
measurements
of
pulsated
ﬂames (formation of large-scale vortices and
their inﬂuence on combustion process and
phase angles of the ﬂame) enabled the develop-
ment of measures to suppress the reactive ring-
vortices and therewith, to control the formation
of self-sustained combustion instabilities [19].
